body_txt: When people in a sufficiently large hierarchy are promoted because of their competence, the end result will tend to put everyone into a position for which they are not competent. In other words, the cream will rise until it sours. The theory behind the Peter Principle is this: Alice is competent in her position, thus she will be promoted to a higher position because of her competence. Alice may not be competent at that new position because she was better suited for the old position. But this means she is unlikely to be demoted , instead she is ineligible for further promotion and stay in the position she is not competent at. She will then be kept in that position indefinitely, even her skillset would make her excellent somewhere else in the hierarchy. Competent workers will keep being promoted as long as there are open slots above ; resulting in being promoted out of the positions they are competent in but kept in the position they struggle with. Since the only way to stay in a position below the top of the hierarchy indefinitely is to be incompetent, without some intervention the organization will eventually stabilize into leadership that is mostly incompetent . This often results when the skills required to do a job well are very different from those required to manage people doing that job, from the military to education to sales. A classic example is a teacher being promoted into a principal—two jobs that require vastly different skillsets, yet the latter is one of the few obvious career paths for the former. Alternatively, sometimes the very qualities that make a character good at one job are diametrically opposed to the qualities they need in the job they're promoted to, like a charismatic politician whose confidence makes them too headstrong to properly compromise, or a cautious and careful analyst being put in a position of leadership that requires taking decisive action. The Hyper-Competent Sidekick is usually one who is fully aware of where their value lies, and so good at their job they know better than to be promoted . Within a story a character may seek some career advancement , only to find it isn't what they were hoping it would be . After a trial run where they are miserable at everything they have to deal with, they happily go back to what they had before. The name comes from the book by Dr. Laurence J. Peter , which is about this principle and discusses it in about twelve chapters worth of detail. See also Obviously Unqualified . The counterpoint is The Dilbert Principle , which states that incompetent workers will always be promoted first ( into inconsequential middle management positions ), in order to keep them from interfering with the efforts of the competent, and is said to be a reaction to the identification of this trope (but if the competent ones want to be promoted, they'll suffer a Passed-Over Promotion ). Scott Adams noted that victims of the Peter Principle at least knew how to do the jobs of their subordinates. It's a common cause of the Bad Boss , Pointy-Haired Boss , Modern Major General and The Neidermeyer . The Career-Building Blunder is one method of defying this trope. Compare and contrast Brain Drain , Kicked Upstairs (which is often a case of this being done deliberately), and Unfit for Greatness .
